original_abstract:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is a ubiquitous endocrine disruptor found in everyday consumer products. In response to mounting safety concerns and strict new legislation, the manufacturing industry has shifted toward using various BPA analogues. While these substitutes possess the necessary polymer-forming properties, they remain significantly less studied than the original compound. Emerging evidence indicates that several of these analogues mimic BPA's behavior, disrupting the endocrine system by interacting with key nuclear receptors, including the estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocorticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X (PXR) receptors.

Recently, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) issued a pivotal re-evaluation of BPA, slashing the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to just 0.2 ng/kg—a two-million-fold reduction. This drastic shift was prompted by growing evidence of BPA’s toxicity, particularly its ability to compromise immune system integrity. Consequently, we conducted a comprehensive review of the immunomodulatory effects of the most prevalent BPA analogues in our environment. Our findings suggest that these substitutes can interfere with both innate and adaptive immunity. Such disruptions may contribute to a range of immune-mediated conditions, including hypersensitivity, allergies, and significant alterations to the human microbiome.
humanized_abstract:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of the most widely used endocrine-disrupting chemicals in consumer products. Following a growing body of evidence on adverse health effects of BPA and an increasing number of bans and restrictions implemented by governments, the use of BPA alternatives or analogs (BPAs) by manufacturers has increased. Although BPAs replace BPA in its polymer-forming capacity, much less is known about the biological effects of BPAs in comparison to BPA. Emerging data show that a number of BPAs interact with nuclear receptors, including estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocorticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X (PXR) receptors and therefore may mimic BPA activity and elicit endocrine effects.

Recently the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) re-examined BPA and reduced the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to 0.2 ng/kg — a two million-fold reduction. The measure was triggered due to new scientific research that shed more light into BPA's toxic effects and specifically damage to the human immune system. It was imperative to scrutinize further all Bisphenol A (BPA) analogues that are present in the environment. After thoroughly analyzing BPA alternatives, our conclusion was that many of them are still immunomodulatory on both innate and adaptive immune system. Consequences include increased risk of developing immune-related health issues including hypersensitivity reactions and allergies, as well as dysregulation of the human microbiome.

document.input:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is a ubiquitous endocrine disruptor found in everyday consumer products. In response to mounting safety concerns and strict new legislation, the manufacturing industry has shifted toward using various BPA analogues. While these substitutes possess the necessary polymer-forming properties, they remain significantly less studied than the original compound. Emerging evidence indicates that several of these analogues mimic BPA's behavior, disrupting the endocrine system by interacting with key nuclear receptors, including the estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocorticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X (PXR) receptors.

Recently, the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) issued a pivotal re-evaluation of BPA, slashing the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to just 0.2 ng/kg—a two-million-fold reduction. Such disruptions may contribute to a range of immune-mediated conditions, including hypersensitivity, allergies, and significant alterations to the human microbiome.
